name: Central Nahuatl
notes: Central Nahuatl (Nāhuatl), a Uto-Aztecan (Nahuan) language cluster of central Mexico, transcribed here in the traditional Hispanic-based ('Jesuit'/classical) orthography used by WikiPron. Grapheme rules: ⟨c⟩ = /k/ but /s/ before a front vowel (⟨e i⟩); ⟨qu⟩ = /k/, ⟨cu⟩ and word-final ⟨uc⟩ = the labialised /kʷ/; ⟨z⟩ = /s/; ⟨x⟩ = /ʃ/; ⟨ch⟩ = /t͡ʃ/; ⟨tz⟩ = /t͡s/; ⟨tl⟩ = the lateral affricate /t͡ɬ/; ⟨hu⟩ = /w/; ⟨ll⟩ = geminate /lː/; ⟨h⟩ = the glottal stop /ʔ/ (the saltillo). Vowels ⟨a e i o u⟩. Latin letters ⟨b d g r f⟩ occur only in Spanish loans. VOWEL LENGTH is phonemic in Nahuatl but is NOT written in this orthography, so the long vowels of the gold (⟨acatl⟩ [aːkat͡ɬ]) cannot be recovered from the spelling and are under-generated; coda ⟨l⟩ → [ɬ] and ⟨uh⟩ → [ɸ] are allophonic and likewise not resolved.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Nahuatl — Phonology and Orthography (classical/Hispanic spelling)
sources.notes: Consonant inventory including /t͡ɬ/, /kʷ/, the saltillo /ʔ/, and the traditional orthography (c, qu, cu, z, x, ch, tz, tl, hu, ll).

sources.author: Andrews, J. Richard
sources.title: Introduction to Classical Nahuatl (Revised Edition)
sources.notes: Standard reference for classical Nahuatl phonology, vowel length and orthography.

ancestors.notes: Uto-Aztecan — the classification node this language sits under.

location.notes: Glottolog's representative point for Central Nahuatl (Puebla-Tlaxcala highlands).
